feedback.text: The DFA offers an opportunity to consolidate the EUs commitment to consumer protection and fundamental rights in the digital environment. To be effective, the DFA should integrate safety, understood as a substantive expression of fairness, while ensuring coherence within the broader EU digital acquis by explicitly aligning with existing instruments such as the DSA, DMA, GDPR, and AI Act. Fragmentation among these frameworks undermines enforcement and legal certainty, creating space for unsafe practices. Thus, uniform treatment of manipulative design and exploitative targeting across instruments would uphold the integrity of the internal market and the principle of a high level of consumer protection stemming from Article 38 of the CFREU. Moreover, the DFA's definitions should be formulated in a technologically-neutral and outcome-oriented manner, referring to the effect of conduct on user autonomy rather than specific technical mechanisms. This ensures adaptability to evolving technology and prevents legal obsolescence. The DFAs effectiveness would also benefit from applying proportionate obligations grounded in the actual risk of rights interference. Proportionality in this sense preserves innovation while safeguarding consumer autonomy, ensuring that regulation targets conduct that substantially endangers safety and fundamental rights rather than imposing general burdens on digital service providers. Consequently, regulation should distinguish between ordinary commercial practices and those that manipulate choice, obscure costs, or exploit vulnerabilities. That said, transparency obligations alone cannot guarantee fairness when excessive information creates confusion. Disclosures should be comprehensible and actionable, enabling users to make informed and thus safe decisions. In essence, safety should function as the core concept in the DFA. The digital space is prone to unsafe design, which is inherently unfair as it diminishes both autonomy and trust of the users. Integrating safety as a constituting principle would achieve three objectives: 1) protection of fundamental rights, embedded in Articles 7, 8, and 38 of the CFREU, by ensuring that manipulative or addictive systems do not erode privacy or human dignity, 2) recognition that safety fosters trust, participation, and sustainable innovation across the internal market, and 3) regulation that is rights-based and, at the same time, focuses on practices and sectors that present compelling risks, such as platforms targeted to youth or personalised advertising. Therefore, safety would bridge fairness and innovation through defining the threshold where design freedom must yield to accountability. The DFAs objectives will be nonetheless unattainable without a coherent and preventive enforcement framework. Synchronisation among DSA Coordinators, data protection authorities, and consumer protection bodies should be formalised to avoid overlapping responsibilities and ensure consistency, while the role of the latest should be central. Furthermore, supervision should emphasise ex ante prevention through design risk assessments and periodic reporting. Such anticipatory oversight is essential to detect unsafe practices before they evolve into systemic harm and to translate the EUs rights-based objectives into practice. The DFA should reaffirm that ensuring fairness in the digital economy is inseparable from ensuring safety. A comprehensive and forward-looking approach has the potential to harmonise consumer protection across the EUs digital acquis, ensure proportional obligations that preserve innovation, embed safety as a substantive component of fairness, as well as establish preventive and coordinated enforcement. In conclusion, through the measures proposed above, the DFA will strengthen trust and safeguard users autonomy, furthering the EUs endeavours to ensure rights-based digital governance.
